label: Monitoring, MLOps & Evaluation Metrics — Part A
topic: Domain 1: Fundamentals of AI and ML

questions.stem: What is data drift in the context of model monitoring?

options.text: Significant changes to the data distribution compared to the data used for training, causing model performance degradation
Data being physically moved from one AWS Region to another
A planned update to the training dataset
The model learning new patterns from production data

explanation.correct: Data drift occurs when there are significant changes to the data distribution compared to training data. Any kind of drift (data or concept) results in model performance degradation.

distractors.B: Data drift is about statistical distribution changes, not physical data movement between Regions.
distractors.C: Planned updates are intentional. Drift is unplanned deviation from training data distributions.
distractors.D: Production models don't learn in real-time. Drift means incoming data no longer matches what the model was trained on.

questions.stem: What is concept drift?

options.text: When the properties of the target variables change, causing the model's learned patterns to become invalid
When data scientists change the model's algorithm
When the model is deployed to a different endpoint
When new features are added to the training data

explanation.correct: Concept drift is when the properties of the target variables change — the relationship between inputs and outputs shifts. The model's learned patterns become invalid for the new reality.

distractors.B: Algorithm changes are intentional model updates. Concept drift is an environmental shift in the underlying patterns.
distractors.C: Endpoint changes are infrastructure. Concept drift is about the target variable's properties changing.
distractors.D: Adding features is feature engineering. Concept drift means the meaning of existing targets has shifted.

questions.stem: In a confusion matrix, what is a false positive?

options.text: When the model predicts positive (e.g., 'is a fish') but the actual value is negative (it is not a fish)
When the model correctly predicts a positive result
When the model correctly predicts a negative result
When the model predicts negative but the actual value is positive

explanation.correct: A false positive is when the model incorrectly predicts positive (e.g., predicts 'fish') but the actual value is negative (not a fish). The model falsely claims something is positive.

distractors.B: A correct positive prediction is a true positive, not a false positive.
distractors.C: A correct negative prediction is a true negative.
distractors.D: Predicting negative when actual is positive is a false negative.

questions.stem: What does the precision metric measure?

options.text: How well the model predicts true positives out of all the positives it identifies — minimizes false positives
How well the model identifies all actual positives — minimizes false negatives
The total percentage of correct predictions
The ratio of false positives to true negatives

explanation.correct: Precision = TP / (TP + FP). It measures how many of the positive predictions were actually correct. It's the right metric when minimizing false positives is the goal (e.g., not labeling legitimate email as spam).

distractors.B: Identifying all actual positives (minimizing false negatives) is recall, not precision.
distractors.C: Total correct predictions is accuracy, not precision.
distractors.D: FP to TN ratio is the false positive rate, not precision.

questions.stem: When should you prioritize the recall metric?

options.text: When you want to minimize false negatives — for example, ensuring you don't miss diagnosing someone who has a disease
When you want to minimize false positives
When you want to maximize overall accuracy
When you want to reduce compute costs

explanation.correct: Recall = TP / (TP + FN). Prioritize recall when false negatives are costly — e.g., missing a disease diagnosis. You want to catch all positive cases even if some negatives are incorrectly flagged.

distractors.B: Minimizing false positives is precision's goal, not recall's.
distractors.C: Accuracy measures all predictions. Recall specifically targets catching all positives.
distractors.D: Recall is a quality metric, not a cost metric.

questions.stem: What are key benefits of MLOps? (Select THREE)

options.text: Productivity — automation and self-service environments let data scientists move faster
Repeatability — automating all steps ensures consistent training, evaluation, and deployment
Auditability — versioning all inputs and outputs enables compliance and lineage tracking
Eliminating the need for any model monitoring
Removing the need for training data
Making all models perfectly accurate

explanation.correct: MLOps benefits include productivity (automation/self-service), repeatability (consistent automated processes), and auditability (versioning everything for compliance). It also improves reliability and data/model quality.

distractors.D: MLOps includes monitoring as a key principle, not eliminates it.
distractors.E: MLOps automates the pipeline including data handling. It doesn't remove the need for data.
distractors.F: MLOps improves process quality but can't guarantee perfect accuracy.

questions.stem: Which are key principles of MLOps? (Select THREE)

options.text: Version control for all artifacts including training data
Monitoring deployments to detect potential issues
Automating re-training because of issues or data/code changes
Manual deployment of all models without automation
Avoiding version control to keep things simple
Never re-training models after initial deployment

explanation.correct: Key MLOps principles: version everything (data, code, models), monitor deployments for issues, and automate re-training triggered by issues or changes. These ensure reliability and compliance.

distractors.D: MLOps emphasizes automation over manual processes for consistency and speed.
distractors.E: Version control is critical in MLOps for tracking lineage and inspecting past configurations.
distractors.F: Automated re-training is a core MLOps principle, not avoidance of it.

questions.stem: What does Amazon SageMaker Model Monitor do? (Select TWO)

options.text: Monitors models in production and detects errors by comparing data against baselines
Sends results to CloudWatch where you can configure alarms to trigger remedial actions like re-training
Automatically fixes all model errors without human intervention
Trains new models from scratch when drift is detected
Replaces the model with a rule-based system when errors are found

explanation.correct: Model Monitor detects errors by comparing production data against baselines using defined rules. Results go to Studio and CloudWatch, where alarms can trigger remedial actions (like starting re-training).

distractors.C: Model Monitor detects issues and alerts. Fixing requires human decisions or automated pipelines, not automatic correction.
distractors.D: Monitor detects drift but doesn't train models. Re-training is triggered by alerts, handled by training pipelines.
distractors.E: Monitor identifies issues. Replacing with rule-based systems would be an architectural change, not an automatic Monitor action.

questions.stem: What does Amazon SageMaker Pipelines provide? (Select TWO)

options.text: Orchestration of SageMaker jobs to author reproducible ML pipelines with all steps to build and deploy a model
Tracking artifact lineage through a simple interface
Physical hardware provisioning for data centers
Manual labeling of training datasets
Creating marketing materials for ML products

explanation.correct: SageMaker Pipelines orchestrates SageMaker jobs into reproducible ML pipelines (containing all build/deploy steps with conditional branches). It also tracks artifact lineage through a simple interface.

distractors.C: Pipelines is a software orchestration service, not hardware provisioning.
distractors.D: Labeling is handled by Ground Truth. Pipelines orchestrates workflow steps.
distractors.E: Marketing is a business function. Pipelines automates ML workflow orchestration.

questions.stem: Which AWS services can orchestrate ML pipelines? (Select TWO)

options.text: AWS Step Functions — visual drag-and-drop workflow builder for serverless workflows
Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow — managed Airflow for scheduling and monitoring workflows
Amazon S3 — stores training data in buckets
Amazon CloudFront — delivers content to edge locations
Amazon RDS — stores relational data

explanation.correct: Besides SageMaker Pipelines, AWS Step Functions (visual workflows integrating AWS services) and Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (managed orchestration) can orchestrate ML pipelines.

distractors.C: S3 stores data. It doesn't orchestrate workflows or pipeline steps.
distractors.D: CloudFront is a CDN. It doesn't orchestrate ML workflows.
distractors.E: RDS is a database. It doesn't manage pipeline orchestration.

questions.scenario: A medical screening model needs to balance catching all disease cases (recall) with not alarming too many healthy patients (precision). The team needs a single metric that combines both.
questions.stem: Which metric balances precision and recall into a single value?

options.text: F1 score — combines precision and recall, useful when both false positives and false negatives matter
Accuracy — the total percentage of correct predictions
AUC — the area under the receiver operating characteristic curve
MSE — the mean squared error

explanation.correct: The F1 score balances precision and recall by combining them in a single metric. It's the best compromise when both false positives and false negatives are important to minimize.

distractors.B: Accuracy measures overall correctness but doesn't specifically balance precision vs recall.
distractors.C: AUC evaluates across all thresholds. F1 specifically balances the precision-recall tradeoff at a given threshold.
distractors.D: MSE is for regression problems (continuous values), not classification precision/recall balance.

questions.scenario: A data scientist is evaluating a logistic regression model that outputs probabilities. They need a metric that evaluates performance across all possible decision thresholds.
questions.stem: Which metric provides an aggregated measure of model performance across the full range of thresholds?

options.text: AUC (Area Under the Curve) — plots true positive rate against false positive rate for increasing thresholds and measures the area under the resulting ROC curve
Precision — measures true positives out of all predicted positives
Recall — measures true positives out of all actual positives
RMSE — measures the square root of mean squared errors

explanation.correct: AUC plots TPR vs FPR across all threshold values on the ROC curve and measures the total area. A score of 1 is perfect; 0.5 is no better than random. It aggregates performance across all possible decision thresholds.

distractors.B: Precision operates at a single threshold. AUC evaluates across all thresholds.
distractors.C: Recall also operates at a single threshold point. AUC provides the aggregate measure.
distractors.D: RMSE is for regression models. AUC evaluates binary classifiers across threshold ranges.

questions.scenario: A team builds a regression model to predict house prices. They need a metric where the units match the predicted variable (dollars) for easy interpretation.
questions.stem: Which metric should they use?

options.text: RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error) — its units match the dependent variable, making it easier to interpret than MSE
MSE — mean squared error with squared units
F1 score — balances precision and recall
AUC — area under the ROC curve

explanation.correct: RMSE is the square root of MSE, so its units match the dependent variable (dollars in this case). If predicting height in inches, RMSE is in inches while MSE is in square inches.

distractors.B: MSE has squared units (square dollars), making it harder to interpret. RMSE resolves this.
distractors.C: F1 is for classification. House price prediction is regression requiring error metrics.
distractors.D: AUC evaluates classifiers. Regression models use error metrics like RMSE.

questions.scenario: A model predicts delivery times. The team doesn't want large errors to dominate the evaluation metric because occasional outliers (traffic jams) are expected and shouldn't disproportionately affect the score.
questions.stem: Which metric averages errors without emphasizing large outliers?

options.text: Mean Absolute Error (MAE) — averages the absolute values of errors without squaring, so large errors don't dominate
MSE — squares errors, emphasizing large ones
RMSE — also emphasizes outliers through squaring
Accuracy — measures classification correctness

explanation.correct: MAE averages absolute error values without squaring. Unlike MSE/RMSE which square errors (emphasizing outliers), MAE treats all errors proportionally — a good choice when outliers shouldn't dominate.

distractors.B: MSE squares errors, making large errors disproportionately impactful. MAE avoids this.
distractors.C: RMSE also emphasizes outliers through the squaring step (before taking the root). MAE doesn't square.
distractors.D: Accuracy is for classification. Delivery time prediction needs regression error metrics.

questions.scenario: An ML model has been in production for 6 months. The VP asks whether the investment has been worthwhile compared to the original business case.
questions.stem: How should the team evaluate the business value of the deployed model?

options.text: Compare actual results against original business goals, calculate ROI by comparing model costs with business value gained, and use cost allocation tags in AWS Cost Explorer
Only look at model accuracy metrics without considering business impact
Compare the model's parameter count to competitors' models
Measure how many training jobs were run during development

explanation.correct: After deployment, compare actual results with original business goals (cost reduction, sales increase, customer improvement). Calculate ROI by comparing model operating costs (tracked via cost allocation tags) with value gained.

distractors.B: Technical accuracy alone doesn't show business value. Business metrics (revenue, cost savings) are needed.
distractors.C: Parameter count is a model characteristic, not a business value metric.
distractors.D: Number of training jobs measures development effort, not business impact of the deployed model.

questions.stem: Which are examples of business metrics for evaluating ML model value? (Select THREE)

options.text: Cost reduction achieved by automating previously manual processes
Percentage increase in users or sales attributed to the model
Measurable improvement in customer feedback or satisfaction
Number of GPU hours consumed during training
Model parameter count in billions
Number of S3 buckets created

explanation.correct: Business metrics include cost reduction, percentage increase in users/sales, and measurable improvement in customer feedback. These quantify the value of ML to the business, not just technical performance.

distractors.D: GPU hours are a cost metric, not a business value metric.
distractors.E: Parameter count is a model characteristic. Business value is measured by outcomes.
distractors.F: S3 bucket count is infrastructure, not business value.

questions.scenario: An MLOps engineer is setting up monitoring for a deployed model. They need to configure the system to detect and respond to issues.
questions.stem: What must a model monitoring system do? (Select THREE)

options.text: Capture production data and compare it to the training set baseline
Define rules to detect issues like data and concept drift
Send alerts when issues are detected
Automatically retrain the model without any human approval
Delete the model when errors are found
Replace the training data with production data automatically

explanation.correct: Monitoring must: capture data and compare to training baseline, define rules for detecting issues (drift), and send alerts when issues are found. This enables timely remedial actions.

distractors.D: Monitoring detects issues and alerts. Retraining should go through an approval process, not be fully automatic without oversight.
distractors.E: Models aren't deleted when errors are found. They're retrained, updated, or investigated.
distractors.F: Production data augments training data through a controlled process, not automatic replacement.

questions.scenario: A finance team wants to track the actual AWS costs of their ML project to calculate ROI.
questions.stem: How can ML project costs be tracked on AWS? (Select TWO)

options.text: Define cost allocation tags with the project name and assign them to all pipeline resources
Filter cost reports in AWS Cost Explorer to determine actual charges for the tagged project
AWS automatically separates ML costs from all other costs without any tagging
Costs can only be estimated and never precisely tracked
Cost tracking requires a third-party tool and cannot be done natively in AWS

explanation.correct: Define cost allocation tags (project name as key, project value as value) and assign to all resources. Then filter AWS Cost Explorer reports to determine actual charges for the project.

distractors.C: AWS doesn't automatically separate project costs. You must define and apply tags for project-level tracking.
distractors.D: With proper tagging, actual AWS charges can be precisely tracked through Cost Explorer.
distractors.E: AWS Cost Explorer with tags provides native cost tracking. No third-party tool is required.

questions.scenario: A binary classification model outputs a probability of 0.72 that an image is of a fish. The team has set a decision threshold of 0.6.
questions.stem: How will the model classify this image?

options.text: As a fish — because the probability (0.72) exceeds the threshold (0.6)
As not a fish — because the probability must be 1.0 to classify as positive
The model cannot make a decision at this probability
As uncertain — a probability of 0.72 is in an undecidable range

explanation.correct: A threshold converts probability into a binary decision. When the threshold is 0.6, any probability at or above 0.6 classifies as positive (fish). Since 0.72 > 0.6, it's classified as a fish.

distractors.B: The threshold is 0.6, not 1.0. Any probability ≥ 0.6 triggers a positive classification.
distractors.C: The threshold makes decisions deterministic. 0.72 clearly exceeds 0.6.
distractors.D: Thresholds produce binary decisions, not uncertainty ranges. Above threshold = positive, below = negative.

questions.scenario: A spam email filter team notices that increasing recall (catching more spam) also increases the number of legitimate emails incorrectly flagged as spam.
questions.stem: What fundamental tradeoff does this illustrate?

options.text: The precision-recall tradeoff — optimizing for one reduces the other because catching more positives also catches more false positives
The cost-accuracy tradeoff — better models always cost more
The speed-quality tradeoff — faster inference reduces accuracy
The size-performance tradeoff — larger models are always less precise

explanation.correct: There is a fundamental tradeoff between precision and recall. Increasing recall (catching all spam) increases false positives (flagging legitimate email). You can't optimize for both simultaneously.

distractors.B: Cost and accuracy are related but this scenario specifically demonstrates precision vs recall, not cost.
distractors.C: Speed-quality is a separate concern. The scenario is about which errors you're willing to accept.
distractors.D: Model size doesn't directly cause this tradeoff. It's inherent to all binary classifiers regardless of size.
